name: Brioche
classification_notes: A revival of 19th-century display type with swash characters and a newly designed italic, placing it firmly in the decorative display serif tradition.
summary: A revival of 19th-century display type with added swash characters and a companion italic designed from scratch by Jessica Hische.
description: The roman draws from a handful of 19th-century specimens, augmented with swashy alternate characters that reflect Hische's lettering background. The italic is an original design rather than a sloped version of the roman. Suited to packaging, branding and editorial contexts that call for decorative flair without straying too far from historical precedent.
foundry_blurb: Brioche is described by Jessica Hische as a fun revival typeface ready for fancy needs. The roman is modelled after 19th-century samples with the addition of swashy special characters, while the italic is an entirely new design.

name: Tilda
classification_notes: A hand-lettered script with intentional naïveté, inspired by film title lettering; sits in the casual script category owing to its informal, slightly raw character despite its formal presentation.
summary: A hand-lettered script designed for the Wes Anderson film Moonrise Kingdom, available in two size-specific cuts to preserve its delicate, intentionally naïve qualities.
description: Created for Moonrise Kingdom's title lettering, this script draws on the quaint aesthetic of Wes Anderson and the 1969 French film La Femme Infidèle. It carries a formally dressed quality while retaining a deliberate rawness. Unusually for a script typeface, it offers two size-specific styles, one for display use and one for smaller settings, maintaining optical consistency across contexts.
foundry_blurb: Jessica Hische describes Tilda as a typeface that set the tone for a sweet film about youthful innocence, inspired by Wes Anderson's quaint aesthetic and the title lettering from La Femme Infidèle (1969). Formally dressed yet openly naïve, it comes in two size-specific styles to preserve its delicate character for both large and small uses, whether in print or on screen.

name: Minot
classification_notes: A layered display typeface designed for multi-colour headline use, with three stylistic variants (Outline, Fill, Box) intended to be overlaid for chromatic effects. Its script-adjacent decorative character places it firmly in the display category.
summary: A three-style layered display typeface by Jessica Hische, designed for chromatic headline work across stationery, labels and celebratory print.
description: Released in three complementary styles, Outline, Fill and Box, Minot is built for stacking into multi-colour compositions rather than use in isolation. The name draws from the French word for child, nodding to its natural fit for baby announcements and occasion stationery alongside wedding invitations and wine labels. Alternate characters extend its decorative range and Hische provides a PDF guide for working with the font's layering system.
foundry_blurb: Minot is a display typeface available in three styles, Outline, Fill and Box, which can be used separately or layered together to create multi-colour headlines. Jessica Hische describes it as perfect for wedding invites, baby announcements, wine labels, and anything you want to make fancy, noting that the name means 'child' in French.

name: Snowflake
classification_notes: A display typeface built around cut paper snowflake forms, with ornamental characters included alongside the full alphabet. Its highly decorative, craft-derived construction places it firmly in the decorative display category.
summary: A cut-paper snowflake-inspired display typeface with a full character set and ornamental glyphs suited to borders and patterns.
description: Built around the aesthetic of cut paper snowflakes, this display typeface includes a full Latin alphabet, numerals, accented characters, punctuation and a set of snowflake ornaments for borders or repeat patterns. The letterforms share visual kinship with crochet work and papel picado, giving it range beyond purely seasonal use. It appeared on Wilco's Cruel Country album artwork.
foundry_blurb: Jessica Hische describes Snowflake as a charming typeface based on cut paper snowflakes, noting its similarity to crochet work and papel picado, which makes it useful across any season. The font includes a full alphabet, numbers, accent characters, punctuation and snowflake ornaments, and was used on Wilco's Cruel Country album.

name: Silencio Sans
classification_notes: Designed for a Hollywood-themed invitation with inspiration drawn from silent film title credits, Silencio Sans is a display-oriented sans with a decorative, period-inflected character rather than a workhorse text face.
summary: A single-weight display sans drawn from silent film title credits, originally made for a Creative Artists Agency Hollywood-themed party invitation in 2014.
description: Commissioned for a CAA Friday Night Party invitation with an old Hollywood theme, the typeface takes its name from the silent film era and draws its forms from movie title credits of that period. Despite its origins, it reads equally well in magazines, invitations and premium packaging. Jessica Hische describes it as a supporting-role face, well suited to pairing with custom lettering or another display face rather than carrying a layout alone.
foundry_blurb: Jessica Hische developed Silencio Sans in 2014 for a Creative Artists Agency party invitation themed around old Hollywood. The name references silent cinema, and the letterforms are drawn from movie title credits of that era. She describes it as a 'best supporting actress' typeface, best paired with custom lettering or another display face, and notes it would feel at home in magazines, invitations and fancy food packaging.

name: Buttermilk
classification_notes: A hand-lettered casual script with lush, flowing forms. Described by the designer as luscious and playful, placing it firmly in the casual script category rather than formal calligraphic scripts.
summary: Jessica Hische's debut typeface from 2008, a flowing casual script with a hand-lettered warmth suited to invitations and packaging.
description: Designed by Jessica Hische in 2008, Buttermilk is a single-weight casual script with lush, rounded letterforms typical of her signature lettering style. It has seen use across a wide range of applications, from wedding stationery to official Barbie holiday packaging. A single desktop and web licence covers most commercial needs, with tiered install options available.
foundry_blurb: Jessica Hische describes Buttermilk as her first typeface, designed in 2008, calling it luscious and playful. She notes it has appeared in countless applications ranging from wedding invitations to official Holiday Barbie packaging.
